page: Google Cloud Platform c3d-highmem-30 is a memory-optimized server instance powered by an AMD EPYC 9B14 processor using the x86_64 architecture. It features 30 dedicated vCPUs mapped to 15 physical cores with multithreading enabled. The instance is configured with 240 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU. No local storage or GPU accelerators are bundled with this instance.

Benchmark evaluations indicate strong single-core performance, highlighted by a stress-ng single-core score of 2866.78 and a strong PassMark memory score of 2793.86. Text processing and small-model LLM text generation also rank in the top tier. However, multi-core CPU performance, database operations, and web serving throughput fall into the average range. A notable performance bottleneck is the large-block memory write bandwidth, which registers in the bottom 25% at 24.31 GB/sec, contrasting with the average read bandwidth of 72.14 GB/sec.

Given its high memory density and dedicated AMD EPYC cores, this instance offers qualitative cost efficiency for memory-bound workloads that do not require local NVMe storage or GPU acceleration. It is well-suited for in-memory databases, caching layers, text processing pipelines, and moderate-scale LLM inference tasks.
description: The Google Cloud Platform c3d-highmem-30 is an x86_64 memory-optimized server instance featuring 30 dedicated vCPUs and 240 GB of RAM, powered by an AMD EPYC 9B14 processor. It provides a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8 GB per core, with no local storage or GPU accelerators included. Performance benchmarks show strong single-core CPU execution and memory subsystem scores, alongside average multi-core and database processing capabilities. However, large-block memory write bandwidth is a relative bottleneck, performing in the lower tier. This instance is designed for memory-intensive applications such as in-memory caching, text processing, and moderate-scale language model inference where high memory capacity per core is required without the need for local physical disks.
